title: Oxidized Oil on Polyester — Sequential Solvent & Alkaline Treatment
stainChemistry: Oxidized cooking oil on polyester undergoes the same polymerization as on cotton (free-radical oxidation and cross-linking of triglycerides), but polyester's thermoplastic, hydrophobic nature makes the polymer bond differently. The polyurethane polymer has weaker affinity for polyester than for cellulose, but polyester's lower water absorbency means aqueous alkaline treatment penetrates more slowly. The oxidized oil appears as a dark brown, sticky, waxy residue on the fiber surface.
whyThisWorks: Oxidized oil on polyester requires a two-phase approach: first, a volatile solvent (VDS) to partially dissolve and lift the polymer off the surface, then aqueous alkaline treatment (dilute KOH) to saponify and hydrolyze remaining polymer residues, finally solvent extraction to remove saponified soap. The combination is necessary because polyester's hydrophobic nature resists aqueous treatment, but solvent alone may not fully degrade the polymerized structure.

spottingProtocol.agent: volatile dry-clean solvent (VDS: perchloroethylene or Stoddard solvent)
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, glass or ceramic container, gentle pressure
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge VDS into the oxidized oil stain from the back side. Work gently and carefully — excessive solvent can damage polyester dyes. The polymerized oil will gradually soften and lighten as the solvent penetrates. Replace the under-pad as it absorbs lifted polymer. This first step dissolves and lifts the bulk of the oxidized oil.
Allow the treated area to air dry completely. VDS will evaporate, leaving the remaining oxidized oil loosened and partially removed. Good ventilation is essential.

spottingProtocol.agent: potassium hydroxide solution (0.5% KOH in water — very dilute, 1 oz per 200 oz water)
spottingProtocol.technique: extremely gentle sponging with careful monitoring
spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, glass or ceramic container, wooden rod, pH paper
spottingProtocol.instruction: Prepare VERY DILUTE KOH solution (half the concentration used on cotton). Polyester is more vulnerable to alkaline damage than cotton. Sponge very gently into any remaining oxidized oil residue from the back side. Keep the area barely moist — do NOT saturate. The residual polymerized oil will gradually saponify. Use pH paper frequently — stop as soon as pH reaches 8-9. Over-alkalinity will damage polyester.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting bottle, pH paper
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 4-6 minutes (multiple rinses until pH is neutral)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with distilled water to remove all KOH residue. This is CRITICAL on polyester — alkaline residue causes yellowing and weakens thermoplastic fiber. Use pH paper to verify neutrality. Perform multiple complete rinses until pH is 6.5-7.5 (neutral). Do NOT skip this step.

spottingProtocol.equipment: white terry pad, spotting board, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place on spotting board and allow to air dry completely in a ventilated area. NEVER use heat on polyester after alkaline treatment — polyester is thermoplastic and heat will set any residual stain or alkali residue into the fiber. Natural air drying is essential.

escalation: If oxidized oil stain persists after the full protocol (VDS + dilute KOH + rinse), the polymer may be extremely cross-linked or the stain may have partially set. Stop — do not repeat, especially do not increase KOH concentration. The risk of polyester damage exceeds the benefit. Professional treatment with professional-grade alkaline systems is the only remaining option, but polyester damage risk is significant.
commonMistakes: Using standard detergent spotting on oxidized oil — will not work because oil is polymerized.
Using full-strength KOH instead of dilute — polyester will be damaged.
Not rinsing thoroughly after KOH — alkaline residue causes yellowing and weakens thermoplastic fiber.
Using heat to dry — polyester will trap alkaline residue and set the stain.
Assuming oxidized oil and fresh oil have the same treatment — fundamentally different chemistry.
scienceNote: Polyester's thermoplastic nature is critical here. Unlike cellulose, polyester has no hydrogen bonding network to buffer against alkaline attack. The PET polymer backbone is vulnerable to hydrolysis at high pH. This is why KOH concentration must be diluted on polyester (0.5% vs. 1% on cotton) and why extensive rinsing and neutral pH verification are absolutely essential. The interplay between polymer cross-linking (oxidized oil) and thermoplastic fiber (polyester) requires understanding both polymer chemistry and fiber chemistry.
safety: Potassium hydroxide is hazardous — causes severe chemical burns. ALWAYS wear heavy nitrile gloves and eye protection. Use glass or ceramic containers only (KOH reacts with metal). Work in ventilated area. In case of contact, flush with water for 15 minutes and seek medical attention. Hydrogen peroxide 3% is safe.
materialWarnings: Polyester is vulnerable to strong alkali — NEVER use concentrated KOH.
Even dilute KOH can damage polyester if left too long or if not rinsed completely.
Polyester dyes can be affected by strong solvents and alkali — test on seams first.
Do NOT use heat after treatment — polyester is thermoplastic.
Alkaline residue on polyester causes yellowing and fiber weakening.
callPro: For colored or valuable polyester garments, professional treatment is strongly recommended. KOH handling requires careful training and proper safety equipment. Professional dry cleaners can use specialized alkaline systems with precise pH control in controlled environments.
customerExplanation: Oxidized cooking oil has polymerized into a cross-linked polymer that standard spotting cannot remove. We first used volatile dry-clean solvent to lift and dissolve the bulk of the polymerized oil. Then we applied very dilute potassium hydroxide to break down remaining polymer chains through saponification. The garment was thoroughly rinsed to neutral pH to remove all alkaline residue (which would damage polyester). Air drying was used to prevent heat from setting any remaining residues.
homeSolutions: OxiClean or oxygen bleach applied as a paste to the stain and left for 20 minutes, then rinsed — weak oxidizing approach that may lighten very fresh oxidized oil slightly, but is unlikely to fully remove it.
Mineral spirits or paint thinner sponged gently into the stain — weaker solvent than professional VDS but may partially dissolve fresh oxidized oil on polyester. Use in ventilated area only.
For extremely fresh oxidized oil (within hours), cold water rinse followed by mild liquid dishwashing detergent may prevent further oxidation, but will not remove polymerized oil.

meta.sourceRef: Expert Garment Restoration & Stain Removal Tips

products.name: Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) — Dilute Solution
products.amazonSearchQuery: potassium hydroxide KOH caustic potash dilute
products.note: HAZARDOUS. For polyester, must be used at 0.5% concentration (half the strength for cotton). Heavy nitrile gloves and eye protection required. Ventilated area. Professional treatment recommended for polyester.
